common_name: Jade Cross Brussels Sprout (AAS Heat-Tolerant F1 Hybrid)

planting.note: Start indoors 12–16 weeks before first fall frost for fall harvest; Jade Cross is an All-America Selections award-winning F1 hybrid Brussels sprout notably more tolerant of warm fall temperatures than heirloom types like Long Island Improved — it sets sprouts at higher temperatures, making it the recommended variety for zones 7–9 where fall temperatures remain warmer longer and standard Brussels sprouts may fail to develop tight, round sprouts.

directions: Start seeds indoors 12–16 weeks before first fall frost at 70–75°F; transplant into rich, firm, well-drained soil with consistent moisture; stake tall plants; when bottom sprouts reach marble size, remove the growing tip ('topping') to concentrate energy; harvest sprouts from the bottom upward when 1–1.5 inches across. Jade Cross is an All-America Selections (AAS) award-winning F1 hybrid Brussels sprout that has been a reliable performer in American gardens since its introduction; it was specifically developed for improved performance in warmer fall conditions compared to the standard heirloom types. Standard Brussels sprouts (Long Island Improved, Catskill, etc.) require consistently cool temperatures (40–60°F) to develop tight, round, high-quality sprouts; in zones 7–9 where fall remains warm well into October, these traditional varieties often produce loose, bitter, or poorly formed sprouts. Jade Cross was selected for earlier sprout initiation at warmer temperatures, allowing it to begin developing sprouts before cool weather arrives and then perfect them as temperatures fall. The sprouts are uniform, round, tightly wrapped, and excellent in flavor — milder and sweeter than heirloom types, with the classic Brussels sprout character that intensifies after frost exposure. Jade Cross is the standard recommendation from Texas A&M AgriLife Extension and Louisiana State University AgCenter for Gulf South Brussels sprout production.

common_name: Oliver Brussels Sprout (Very Early F1 Hybrid)

planting.note: Start indoors 12–16 weeks before first fall frost; Oliver is a very early-maturing F1 Brussels sprout that initiates sprout development earlier in the season than most varieties, making it one of the best choices for short-season northern gardens (zones 3–5) where the growing window is compressed, and for Gulf Coast gardeners who need sprout harvest to complete before heat arrives in spring.

directions: Start seeds indoors 12–16 weeks before first fall frost; transplant into firm, rich, consistently moist soil; stake tall plants against wind; remove the growing tip when bottom sprouts reach marble size to encourage rapid maturing of the rest; harvest from the bottom upward when sprouts are 1–1.5 inches across. Oliver is an F1 hybrid Brussels sprout bred for early sprout initiation — one of the earliest-maturing Brussels sprout varieties commercially available — that produces uniform, round, high-quality sprouts beginning earlier in the fall season than standard types. The relatively compact plants (24–30 inches) with short internodes produce sprouts packed closely on the stem in tight, uniform clusters that are excellent for harvest. The early initiation of sprout development makes Oliver particularly valuable in two challenging situations: in zones 3–5 where the growing season is short and standard Brussels sprouts often don't finish before hard freeze, and in zones 7–9 where the goal is to get sprout harvest completed before the arrival of hot spring weather. Oliver's flavor is mild and sweet, comparable to other modern hybrid Brussels sprouts, with the characteristic improvement in flavor and sweetness after frost exposure. A practical, reliable choice for gardeners who want Brussels sprouts without the 100–120 day wait of heirloom types.

common_name: Falstaff Brussels Sprout (Deep Red-Purple Anthocyanin-Rich F1 Hybrid)

planting.note: Start indoors 14–18 weeks before first fall frost; Falstaff is a striking purple-red Brussels sprout F1 hybrid whose intense color comes from very high anthocyanin concentrations — the same antioxidant pigments in red cabbage and blueberries — that intensify with cold exposure; it is the classic 'purple Brussels sprout' for gardeners who want visual drama alongside the vegetable's traditional nutty, sweet flavor.

directions: Start seeds indoors 14–18 weeks before first fall frost; transplant into rich, firm, well-drained soil; Falstaff needs consistently cool fall temperatures to develop its deepest purple color — color intensifies dramatically after frost; harvest when sprouts are 1–1.5 inches across. Falstaff is the premier purple Brussels sprout for home gardeners and is named for the colorful, exuberant Shakespearean character Sir John Falstaff — the name suggests something robust, larger-than-life, and visually dramatic, which the deep red-purple sprouts certainly are. The intense color comes from very high concentrations of anthocyanin pigments distributed throughout the outer leaves and (to a lesser degree) the interior of each sprout; the color deepens dramatically with cold temperatures, making late-season fall-harvested Falstaff significantly more deeply purple than sprouts harvested in early fall before hard frost. The purple color fades to an unappetizing grayish-green if overcooked in water, but holds better with roasting, steaming, or quick stir-frying — and the raw purple sprouts are spectacularly visual in slaws and salads where the color impact is undiminished. The flavor is equivalent to standard green Brussels sprout varieties: nutty, slightly bitter, sweet — some gardeners find the flavor of purple types slightly milder than green types, possibly because the anthocyanin pigmentation is linked to somewhat lower glucosinolate levels.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil; Corinto is a parthenocarpic (self-pollinating) seedless mini slicer cucumber producing small (5–6 inch), thin-skinned, seedless to nearly seedless fruits without the need for pollinating bees — ideal for high tunnel production, greenhouses, and gardens with limited pollinator activity; fruits remain sweet and non-bitter even when slightly oversized.

directions: Sow directly 1 inch deep and provide a trellis for vertical growing; Corinto is parthenocarpic and sets fruit without pollination, making it more reliable in cool springs or greenhouses where bees are absent; harvest frequently (every 2–3 days) at 5–6 inches for the sweetest quality. Corinto is an F1 parthenocarpic (self-pollinating) cucumber from Seminis/Monsanto Seeds specifically bred for high tunnel and greenhouse production but equally excellent in open garden conditions — the key 'parthenocarpic' characteristic means the plant sets fruit without requiring pollinating bees, an advantage in enclosed structures, in cool early spring conditions when bees are inactive, or in suburban gardens with limited pollinator activity. The fruits are small to medium (5–6 inches), cylindrical, dark green, and nearly seedless with very thin, tender skin that doesn't require peeling — the 'mini seedless cucumber' style increasingly available in grocery stores. The thin skin and near-absence of seeds gives Corinto a different texture from seeded slicing cucumbers — more uniformly crisp and mild throughout without the slightly bitter jelly surrounding seeds. The flavor is mild, sweet, and clean; Corinto stays mild and non-bitter even when slightly over-mature (unlike seeded types which become more bitter as they yellow). Excellent for fresh eating, salads, and pickling whole.

common_name: Bush Pickle Cucumber (Compact Pickling Cucumber for Small Gardens)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil; Bush Pickle is a compact, determinate pickling cucumber that grows as a bush (18–24 inch spread) rather than a long vine, making it ideal for raised beds, containers, and small gardens where standard pickling cucumber vines (4–6 feet) would be unmanageable; it produces a concentrated set of uniform pickling cucumbers and then declines — plant a second succession for extended harvest.

directions: Sow directly 1 inch deep after last frost in warm, fertile, well-drained soil; no trellis required for the compact bush plants; harvest pickling cucumbers at 3–4 inches for small gherkin-style pickles or 4–5 inches for standard dill pickle size; pick every 2–3 days to maintain production. Bush Pickle is a compact, determinate (bush-habit) pickling cucumber developed specifically for small-space gardening — raised beds, containers, urban gardens, and patios where growing the standard trailing pickling cucumber vines (Boston Pickling, National Pickling, Calypso) is impractical due to their 4–6 foot spread. The compact plants (18–24 inches wide) produce a dense cluster of uniform, blocky, 3–5 inch pickling cucumbers with the classic pickling cucumber characteristics: firm, crisp flesh; thin skin; minimal hollow seeds; and a blocky, multi-bumped exterior that is traditional for dill pickles. Production is somewhat concentrated in a shorter harvest window compared to vining types — Bush Pickle tends to set most of its fruit within 2–3 weeks of first harvest, then declines, making succession planting (a second sowing 3–4 weeks after the first) useful for extended pickle production. Still holds well under warm summer conditions for Gulf Coast gardeners growing in small raised beds.
